issue_body: Currently density is the thing which can either push your score to the sky or dump it to the ground. ## Motivation Density is a multiplier which is bounded in `(0, 1.15]` and it's intended to favor small changes over large, but as a side effect it also often makes really small changes score as much as a large feature scores. Even few lines of code may be enough to score much higher than a regular brand-new improvement. In pair with the fact that we still score deleted code the same as newly introduced, it also makes possible to remove a few lines of code to achieve the same results. This question was raised previously and was handled using label multipliers. However none of existing label multipliers are currently capable of recovering the situation. Some examples of few-line edits: - https://gittensor.io/miners/pr?repo=entrius%2Fgittensor&number=1040, +**9** Source LOC (3 of them are comments, so could be even less), **28.97** base score, density **1.15** (capped) - https://gittensor.io/miners/pr?repo=entrius%2Fgittensor&number=1187, -**26** Source LOC, **26.95** base score, density **1.07** (dead code removal) - https://gittensor.io/miners/pr?repo=entrius%2Fgittensor&number=627, +**17** Source LOC, **26.41** base score, density **1.04** - https://gittensor.io/miners/pr?repo=entrius%2Fgittensor&number=827, +**8** Source LOC, **23.11** base score, density **0.92** Which are scored with bigger scores than: - https://gittensor.io/miners/pr?repo=entrius%2Fgittensor&number=947, + **311** Source LOC, **23.09** base score, density **0.76** - Fully qualified feature which has base score lower than ALL few-line edits from above. You may say that label multipliers may do the work, but consider the review quality debuff (which is more usual for meaningful changes than for few-line edits) and consider possible fix/enh label multipliers on few-line fixes as well - and voila, you're lucky if a feature is scored at least on par with what I mentioned above. - https://gittensor.io/miners/pr?repo=entrius%2Fgittensor&number=967, + **22**, -**27** Source LOC, **6.54** base score, density **0.25** - Optimization which is +- the same on size (actually a bit bigger) like PRs mentioned above, but scored x5-x4 times less. - https://gittensor.io/miners/pr?repo=entrius%2Fgittensor&number=1253, **20.16** base score, **0.72** density - The same dead code removal, but across the whole repo. It always would be scored much lower than targeted one-function-drop PR because of density. That's why people prefer to send 5 different PRs with dead code removal rather than remove everything at once - because it will make e.g. 100 total score for them instead of 10 via a single PR. You may say that it's easier to review and land, but still: 1) you may ask to split bigger PRs yourself if you want; 2) it's definitely not fair score calculation. Also density: - Discourages adding/changing (specifically, as line change counts x2: +1 for addition and -1 for deletion) imports (or any other structures which have low token score) - Heavily punishes wrapping existing code with "if" (because it will indent the whole branch and add +N lines changed which doesn't contribute score, but still affect density) - Heavily punishes comments and docstrings - In general it heavily punishes any formatting changes. For example, introducing an argument to a 5-arg function may result in auto-formatted wrapping all args to new lines -> and result in much reduced score because of density. - Has weird `if src_tok <5: base_score = 0` check. It results in: https://gittensor.io/miners/pr?repo=entrius%2Fgittensor&number=1173 (4.73 `src_tok`) scored in **0.08**, but https://gittensor.io/miners/pr?repo=entrius%2Fgittensor&number=1187 (6.23 `src_tok`) scored in **26.95**. x300+ bigger score just for adding +1 to `src_tok`. - And many-many other unfair examples. The real novel code will always be scored less than a targeted few-lines edit with a capped or nearly-capped density. Label multipliers won't help much. ## What I propose As we saw, density makes the resulting score unpredictable. 500-LOC of meaningful changes could be scored in 5.0 as well as in 20.0, depending on actually randomness. Additionally, targeted dense changes may beat any other PR quite easily. I propose to drop density from score calculation and replace it with saturation multiplier based plainly on `src_tokens`. Before: `initial_base_score = MERGED_PR_BASE_SCORE * min(score / lines, 1.15)` After: `initial_base_score = MERGED_PR_BASE_SCORE * (1 - exp(-src_tok / 58))` The new formula is: - Predictable. You know that the more you code -> the more you get. With density you never know how much score you'll get, as it really depends on many-many things. 5 LOC may result in 30.0 base score, 100 LOC may result in 5.0 base score, there're a lot of such examples. Small-but-real work earns small-but-real score. - Doesn't punish you for formatting changes and doesn't punish you for using low-scored structures, like imports. - Much simpler. Besides the density calculation itself, density needed hardcoded `if src_tok < 5: base_score = 0` (why 5? Why not 10?) to suppress noise. The exponential is zero at zero, ruled by one constant: no need for additional density cap threshold and additional min limit for `src_tok`. Old bounds and caps are *mostly* respected: previous multiplier had 1.15 maximum, now it's 1.0. So `initial_base_score` now has 25.0 cap (`MERGED_PR_BASE_SCORE` itself) vs 28.75 previously. `base_score` was capped at 53.75 (practically unreachable actually), now at `50.0`. I think it's actually looks better than before. How we may tune the formula (proposed `SCALE = 58` is highlighted): How it would affect scoring based on PRs from this repo. It's funny how https://gittensor.io/miners/pr?repo=entrius%2Fgittensor&number=1040 (5 LOC of meaningful changes) was mostly on par with #1202 (global project overhaul) @anderdc @LandynDev @entrius wdyt?
